text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - POST-OPERATIVE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

Patient: Renata Okonkwo
Age: 39
Encounter Type: Post-Surgical Follow-Up / Treatment Planning Visit
Attending: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for post-operative evaluation following bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with left axillary lymph node dissection and immediate tissue expander placement. Review of final surgical pathology results and discussion of adjuvant therapy planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 39-year-old premenopausal Nigerian-American woman, BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant carrier (c.5074del, p.Cys1692ValfsTer17, heterozygous), with a diagnosis of stage IIB (cT2 N1 M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, upper outer quadrant, grade 3, Ki-67 85%. She presented today for her post-operative oncology follow-up, approximately 4 weeks following bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with immediate bilateral tissue expander placement and left sentinel lymph node biopsy plus complete left axillary lymph node dissection.

By way of background, Ms. Okonkwo completed the neoadjuvant phase of KEYNOTE-522-based therapy consisting of dose-dense Adriamycin 60 mg/m2 and Cytoxan 600 mg/m2 every 2 weeks for 4 cycles (with pegfilgrastim support) concurrently with Keytruda 200 mg IV every 3 weeks, followed by weekly Taxol 80 mg/m2 (dose-reduced to 64 mg/m2 for Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy from week 6 onward) for 12 weeks with Keytruda every 3 weeks for 4 doses. Total neoadjuvant pembro: approximately 8 doses. The neoadjuvant course was complicated by Keytruda-induced immune thyroiditis (thyrotoxic phase followed by hypothyroid phase), requiring atenolol for symptomatic management and subsequently levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, which she continues. She also developed Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy with bilateral fingertip and toe tingling during the Taxol phase, which prompted the dose reduction. She tolerated the full planned neoadjuvant course.

Pre-surgical restaging MRI of the left breast demonstrated near-complete but not complete radiographic response — residual left breast mass measuring 1.4 x 1.1 cm (baseline 3.4 cm) with heterogeneous non-mass enhancement pattern and a left axillary lymph node still mildly abnormal at 0.9 cm.

Surgery was performed without intraoperative complications. Given her confirmed BRCA1 carrier status, she and the surgical oncology team elected bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy rather than breast-conserving surgery, with simultaneous right prophylactic mastectomy. Left sentinel lymph node biopsy identified 1 sentinel node; complete left axillary dissection yielded 14 additional lymph nodes.

Today, we are reviewing the final surgical pathology in detail and discussing the plan for adjuvant systemic therapy.

Final surgical pathology summary (reviewed in detail today):
- LEFT BREAST: Residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, measuring 1.3 x 0.9 x 0.8 cm with extensive treatment-associated changes (approximately 60-70% necrosis, fibrosis, foamy macrophage infiltrates). Viable tumor nests present. Margins negative, closest 8 mm posterior. Nipple and skin not involved. No lymphovascular invasion.
- LEFT SENTINEL LYMPH NODE (1 node): Isolated tumor cells (0.15 mm cluster, CK AE1/AE3 positive) — classified as ypN0(i+).
- LEFT AXILLARY LYMPH NODES (14 nodes): 2 of 14 nodes with metastatic carcinoma (4 mm and 2 mm deposits). No extranodal extension.
- FINAL ypTNM STAGE: ypT1b ypN1 M0
- RESIDUAL CANCER BURDEN: RCB class II (intermediate residual disease). Non-pathologic complete response (non-pCR).
- Receptor status on residual tumor: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 1+ (IHC) — unchanged.
- RIGHT BREAST (prophylactic): No carcinoma, no DCIS, no atypia.

This represents a partial pathologic response to neoadjuvant therapy. The patient did not achieve a pathologic complete response (pCR), which carries prognostic implications in TNBC. RCB class II confirms intermediate residual disease, associated with significantly worse recurrence-free and overall survival compared to pCR.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Reports fatigue, moderate (Grade 1, improved from Grade 2 during active chemotherapy). Appetite has improved since completing chemotherapy.
Neurologic: Bilateral fingertip tingling persisting, Grade 1 (improved from Grade 2 at its worst during PTX). No motor deficits. No fall risk reported.
Endocrine/Metabolic: No symptoms of hypothyroidism (euthyroid on levothyroxine). No recurrence of thyrotoxic symptoms. Denies palpitations or heat intolerance.
Musculoskeletal: Mild residual bilateral hand and foot soreness (improved since stopping Taxol). No joint swelling.
Surgical site: Left axillary drain was removed approximately 2 weeks post-operatively. Right drain removed at 10 days. Mild wound discomfort, controlled with over-the-counter analgesics. No fever, no erythema at drain sites.
Respiratory: No dyspnea, no cough.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel habits normalizing. No constipation (was present during hypothyroid phase). No nausea since completing chemotherapy.
Skin/Integument: Residual alopecia from chemotherapy; notes early re-growth of scalp hair over the past 2-3 weeks.
Psychosocial: Reports significant emotional distress related to the BRCA1 diagnosis and its implications for her children (she has two children, ages 7 and 10). Feeling anxious about the non-pCR result discussed today. Engaged with oncology social work.
Ophthalmologic: No visual changes.
Urologic/Gynecologic: Normal menstrual cycle has not resumed (chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea). Patient reports she banked embryos prior to initiating chemotherapy; fertility counseling was completed prior to treatment. Notes occasional vaginal dryness.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress. Well-appearing. Mild fatigue noted. Emotional but composed.
Vital Signs: BP 112/72 mmHg, HR 68 bpm (regular), RR 16, T 36.8°C (afebrile), SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 66.2 kg (baseline 68 kg; modest weight loss during treatment, trending toward recovery).
HEENT: No conjunctival pallor. Mild scalp hair regrowth noted, including 2-3 mm fine hair over the vertex. No eyelash regrowth yet.
Neck: Supple. No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular adenopathy palpable.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No rales, no wheezing.
Breast/Chest Wall: Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy incisions well-healed with no signs of infection, dehiscence, or skin flap necrosis. Bilateral tissue expanders palpable in the submuscular position, appropriately positioned. Left axillary dissection incision well-healed. Mild firmness and tenderness over the left anterior chest wall, consistent with post-operative changes. No palpable masses or areas of concerning induration. No evidence of axillary seroma (drains removed, no re-accumulation palpated). Right chest wall without complications.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. No palpable masses.
Extremities: No left upper extremity edema today (early post-operative period; patient educated on lymphedema surveillance). Bilateral fingertips mildly hypesthetic to light touch — consistent with Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy. No motor deficits. No asterixis.
Neurologic: CN II-XII intact. Gait normal. Reflexes symmetric and normal.
Skin: No rash. No palmar-plantar erythema. Hair in early regrowth as above.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC (obtained today, approximately 4 weeks post-surgery):
  - WBC: 6.8 x10^3/uL (normal)
  - ANC: 4,100/uL (recovered)
  - Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, improved from treatment nadir)
  - Hematocrit: 34.5%
  - Platelets: 268 x10^3/uL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine: 0.72 mg/dL, BUN: 12 mg/dL
  - ALT: 24 U/L (normal), AST: 21 U/L (normal), Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L (normal), Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
  - Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
  - Glucose: 88 mg/dL

Thyroid Function:
  - TSH: 3.1 mIU/L (within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily)
  - Free T4: 1.2 ng/dL (normal)
  Interpretation: Thyroid function well-controlled. No medication adjustment needed at this time.

---

IMAGING
Pre-surgical restaging MRI left breast (reviewed, performed prior to surgery): Residual 1.4 x 1.1 cm mass, near-complete radiographic response. Mildly abnormal left axillary node 0.9 cm. No contralateral disease, no distant metastases.

No new imaging performed today. Post-operative staging CT not indicated at this time per NCCN guidelines; surveillance imaging will be planned as part of adjuvant phase monitoring.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 39-year-old BRCA1 carrier with stage IIB triple-negative, grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, now 4 weeks post bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy and left ALND. She has received 8 doses of pembro in the neoadjuvant setting. Final pathology confirms ypT1b ypN1 M0 disease with RCB class II residual disease — non-pCR. She is recovering well with Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (improving), euthyroid on levothyroxine, no active infections, and incisions healing appropriately.

The non-pCR result is clinically significant and warrants intensification of the adjuvant treatment plan beyond standard of care for pCR patients:

1. ADJUVANT THERAPY — Xeloda:
Per CREATE-X trial data (Masuda et al., NEJM 2017), patients with HER2-negative breast cancer (including TNBC) who do not achieve pCR after neoadjuvant chemotherapy derive significant survival benefit from adjuvant Xeloda. In the TNBC subgroup, this benefit was particularly pronounced (5-year DFS improvement of approximately 13%). I discussed these data in detail with Ms. Okonkwo today, including the absolute benefit, expected toxicities, and the rationale for proceeding.

Plan: Xeloda 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily, days 1 through 14 of each 21-day cycle, for 6-8 cycles (we will plan for 8 cycles). BSA: 1.71 m2 (weight 66.2 kg today). Pharmacy-calculated dose: 1710 mg PO BID (rounded per institutional rounding table). To start in approximately 2 weeks (6 weeks post-surgery), pending wound healing confirmation and adequate hematologic recovery.

Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, HFS) prevention counseling provided extensively today:
  - Urea-based cream (e.g., Eucerin or Udderly Smooth) applied to palms and soles twice daily starting day 1 of treatment
  - Avoid constrictive footwear, prolonged standing, and friction-causing activities
  - Keep hands and feet cool and moisturized
  - Avoid hot water exposure (dishes, baths)
  - Report Grade 1 symptoms early so we can intervene before progression to Grade 2-3
  - Written HFS handout provided

Other anticipated cape toxicities reviewed: diarrhea (loperamide PRN per protocol), nausea (ondansetron PRN), fatigue, mucositis. Instructed patient to hold cape and call if Grade 2 or greater toxicity develops.

DHPD deficiency: Patient does not have known DPYD variants (not tested prior to initiation given CREATE-X data and prior treatment; will order DPYD pharmacogenomic testing before the first dose to screen for severe cape toxicity risk).

2. ADJUVANT pembro CONTINUATION (KEYNOTE-522):
Per KEYNOTE-522 protocol, pembro is administered for a total of approximately 1 year from initiation (17 doses total across neoadjuvant and adjuvant phases). Ms. Okonkwo received 8 doses in the neoadjuvant setting. She therefore has approximately 9 additional doses of Keytruda 200 mg IV every 21 days remaining in the adjuvant setting.

Given the thyroiditis that occurred during neoadjuvant Keytruda (well-managed, no Grade 3 irAEs), she is an acceptable candidate to resume pembro in the adjuvant setting. Thyroid function is currently stable. We will restart pembro 200 mg IV every 3 weeks concurrently with Xeloda.

Monitoring plan on adjuvant Keytruda + cape:
  - CBC, CMP, LFTs every 3 weeks prior to each Keytruda dose
  - TSH every 6-8 weeks (ongoing thyroiditis monitoring)
  - Blood pressure monitoring
  - Vigilance for new immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, adrenal insufficiency, etc.)
  - Patient educated on irAE symptoms to monitor and when to call the clinic

3. BRCA1-RELATED ADJUVANT OPTIONS (ola):
Given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and non-pCR after neoadjuvant platinum-free chemotherapy, the patient is potentially eligible for adjuvant ola per the OlympiA trial (Tutt et al., NEJM 2021). OlympiA demonstrated a significant improvement in invasive disease-free survival and overall survival with adjuvant Lynparza 300 mg BID for 1 year in high-risk BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative breast cancer patients.

After careful discussion with the patient today, we are electing to proceed with cape + pembro first, followed by consideration of ola as a subsequent or overlapping option if needed based on disease trajectory and toxicity tolerance. We discussed that Xeloda + pembro and ola have not been directly compared in the adjuvant setting and the possibility of sequential use is reasonable. This decision was made collaboratively with the patient, who is appropriately motivated to maximize survival outcomes while managing quality of life concerns.

Lynparza will be revisited at the end of adjuvant Xeloda, particularly if no recurrence has occurred and Keytruda is complete.

4. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (ONGOING):
Grade 1 bilateral fingertip paresthesia, improving since discontinuation of Taxol. No pharmacologic treatment indicated at this grade. Will continue to monitor. If neuropathy worsens or is significantly impacting quality of life, duloxetine may be considered per ASCO guidelines (limited evidence but some benefit for CIPN).

5. IMMUNE THYROIDITIS / HYPOTHYROIDISM:
Well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 3.1 mIU/L today — no dose adjustment needed. Will continue 6-8 week TSH monitoring while on pembro. She understands to call if she develops recurrence of thyroid symptoms.

6. FERTILITY AND REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH:
Chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea present. Pre-treatment embryo cryopreservation was completed. Discussed that menstrual recovery may occur after stopping chemotherapy, though recovery is variable and not guaranteed at her age. She does not wish to pursue additional pregnancy at this time; both pregnancies are complete per her report. BRCA1 implications for her two children (ages 7 and 10) discussed — genetic testing recommended when they reach adulthood (ages 18-25). Letters for family members provided per genetics program protocol.

7. RISK-REDUCING BILATERAL SALPINGO-OOPHORECTOMY (RRSO):
Given confirmed BRCA1 carrier status, RRSO is strongly recommended per NCCN HBOC guidelines (recommended age 35-40 for BRCA1 carriers). The patient was premenopausal at the time of surgery. We will refer to gynecologic oncology now; however, given ongoing active treatment, RRSO will be scheduled after completing adjuvant systemic therapy to minimize surgical stress during treatment. Target: schedule RRSO for approximately 6-9 months from today (after completing cape and approaching or completing Keytruda adjuvant dosing).

8. SURVIVORSHIP AND PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Ms. Okonkwo reports significant emotional distress related to her diagnosis at age 39, the non-pCR result (discussed openly and honestly today), BRCA1 carrier status and implications for her children, and uncertainty about long-term prognosis. She was tearful during the discussion of the RCB class II result and the rationale for intensified adjuvant therapy. I spent additional time with her providing emotional support, honest prognostic counseling, and emphasizing that her treatment team remains committed to maximizing her long-term outcomes.

Referrals/supports in place:
  - Oncology social work (established, active)
  - Genetic counseling (active, for family cascade testing)
  - Plastic surgery (tissue expander fills on track per plastic surgery team)
  - Physical therapy for left upper extremity lymphedema surveillance (baseline circumferential measurements obtained by PT today)
  - Integrative oncology referral placed (acupuncture, mind-body medicine for anxiety/sleep)

I addressed the patient's specific question today: "Does the non-pCR mean my cancer is likely to come back?" I provided balanced, honest counseling that RCB class II represents an intermediate risk group and that the additional adjuvant therapy we are planning (Xeloda + pembro, and potentially ola) is specifically designed to reduce this residual risk. I also noted that 5-year recurrence-free survival rates in patients like her who go on to receive adjuvant cape after non-pCR are meaningfully improved compared to no additional therapy.

9. FOLLOW-UP:
- Labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH) and visit in 2 weeks to confirm clearance to start Xeloda and pembro
- Keytruda infusion and cape prescription to be initiated at that 2-week visit
- DPYD testing ordered today; results pending before cycle 1
- Routine surveillance: After completing adjuvant therapy, oncology visits every 3-4 months for first 2 years, every 6 months years 3-5, then annually
- Annual chest wall and bilateral reconstructed breast MRI (no standard mammogram given bilateral mastectomy)
- No routine CT surveillance or tumor marker monitoring per ASCO TNBC survivorship guidelines

Time spent with patient today: 55 minutes, greater than 50% in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
